article_title: NVIDIA Launches Halos for Robotics, Porting Its Autonomous-Vehicle Safety Stack to Humanoid and Industrial Robots
summary: Submission approvable with 1 minor warning(s) — file a corrections record on merge

content_preview.title: NVIDIA Launches Halos for Robotics, Porting Its Autonomous-Vehicle Safety Stack to Humanoid and Industrial Robots
content_preview.summary: At Automate 2026, NVIDIA unveiled Halos for Robotics, a three-layer functional-safety system spanning IGX Thor compute, Halos OS, and an ANAB-accredited inspection lab, with Agility Robotics as first adopter.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

NVIDIA used the Automate 2026 trade show in Chicago to introduce Halos for Robotics, which it describes as the industry's first full-stack, comprehensive safety system for robotics and physical AI that unifies AI compute and safety. According to [NVIDIA](https://nvidianews.nvidia.com/news/nvidia-announces-halos-for-robotics-the-industrys-first-full-stack-safety-system-for-physical-ai), the system was announced on June 22, 2026, and adapts the functional-safety foundation the company...

recommendations: Before merging, the human reviewer should write a corrections file at src/content/corrections/<YYYY-MM>/<article-slug>.json documenting the warning(s).
Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Clear, well-structured News piece (884 words, within the 400-1200 range). Strong layering of the three-part stack (IGX Thor + Holoscan Sensor Bridge / Halos OS / AI Systems Inspection Lab), with a clean 'What We Don't Know' section that appropriately flags NVIDIA's 'first' claims as vendor-supplied rather than independently benchmarked.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 4 source snapshots read from disk and verified (no archive fallbacks; all HTTP 200). source-0 (nvidianews.nvidia.com): confirms June 22 2026 announcement date, 'industry's first full-stack, comprehensive safety system... that unifies AI compute and safety' verbatim, the three-layer architecture, Deepu Talla quote verbatim with correct title, Laurie E. Locascio quote verbatim ('president and CEO of ANSI'), Peggy Johnson quote verbatim ('nonnegotiable requirement'), Agility as first adopter with Amazon/GXO/Schaeffler/Toyota Motor Manufacturing Canada customers, availability details (Halos Core early access Linux + QNX OS for Safety 8.0; Outside-In Blueprint open-source on GitHub), and the certifier list (TUV Rheinland, UL Solutions, TUV SUD, exida, SGS, CertX). source-1 (developer.nvidia.com): confirms 'up to 2,070 FP4 TFLOPs', 14x Neoverse Arm cores, 128 GB / 273 GB/s, 'ANAB-accredited ISO/IEC 17020 Inspection Body', 'IEC 61508 SIL 3 capable' Safety Island, 'end-to-end IEC 61508 SIL 2 safety protocol', and 'over 43 companies' verbatim. source-2 (engineering.com): confirms '18,600+ engineering years' verbatim and the IEC 61508 / ISO 13849 / ISO/IEC TR 5469 standards, and 'first to use' Agility framing. source-3 (theaiinsider.tech): confirms 'Agility Robotics is the first company to announce plans to use the platform'. HPCwire is NOT cited (correctly avoided). IGX Thor GA internal cross-reference resolves to the real published article src/content/articles/2026-03/21-nvidia-igx-thor-reaches-general-availability...md.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All technical specifics, named standards, accreditations, quotes, names and the June 22 2026 date trace correctly to the cited sources. One unsourced specific: the Overview lead and the summary state the launch was made at 'the Automate 2026 trade show in Chicago.' None of the four cited sources establish the announcement venue (the newsroom gives only the date; 'Automate' appears in the sources only as an unrelated partner press-release URL and a 'Automated trailer loading' caption). The claim is factually plausible (prior MH coverage establishes Automate 2026 ran in Chicago June 22-25, 2026 per The Robot Report) but is not traceable to this article's cited sources. Filed as a single correction. Minor wording note (not corrected): the article says '22,000 safety mechanisms' where the developer blog says '22,000 platform safety monitors' - substantively equivalent.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional. Vendor 'first' claims are attributed to NVIDIA/ANSI rather than asserted as fact, and the closing section explicitly hedges on independent benchmarking, pricing, certification completion and durability of an NVIDIA-originated inspection certificate.
editor_notes.originality: Original topic. No prior MH article covers Halos for Robotics. Adjacent June 2026 robotics coverage (Kawasaki at Automate 2026, Isaac GR00T, various humanoid stories) is distinct; this is the only piece on NVIDIA's functional-safety stack.
concerns: The Overview lead and the article summary state the launch occurred at 'the Automate 2026 trade show in Chicago,' but none of the four cited sources support the venue. The cited sources establish only the June 22 2026 announcement date. The claim is plausibly true (Automate 2026 ran in Chicago that week) but is not traceable to this article's cited sources, so it is filed as a public correction rather than left unflagged.
recommendations: Allowlist warnings for developer.nvidia.com, engineering.com and theaiinsider.tech are NON-BLOCKING; the maintainer may consider adding developer.nvidia.com (an NVIDIA first-party domain) to config/source_allowlist.txt, but no allowlist edit was made as part of this review.
Future submissions should cite the announcement venue only when a cited source states it, or omit the venue.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Substantively accurate, well-sourced News piece. Every quote, number, standard, accreditation and name verifies verbatim against the cited NVIDIA newsroom and developer-blog snapshots. The single recoverable issue - an unsourced 'Automate 2026 in Chicago' venue in the lead/summary - is honestly covered by a single corrections record. Verdict: APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS.
